title: Getting to Yes
subtitle: Negotiating Agreement Without Giving In
author: Roger Fisher, William Ury, Bruce Patton
themes: Separating people from problems in negotiation
Focusing on interests rather than positions
Using objective criteria to resolve differences
Leveraging BATNA as a source of negotiating power
excerpt: The seminal guide to principled negotiation — separating people from problems, focusing on interests not positions, inventing options for mutual gain, and insisting on objective criteria.
keyIdeas: Separate the people from the problem
Know your BATNA — Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement
keyTakeaways: Negotiation is not about winning or losing — it is about finding a mutually acceptable solution
Attacking positions entrenches them; exploring interests opens possibilities
Your BATNA is your greatest source of negotiating power
Principled negotiation works across cultures, deal sizes, and relationship depths
whoShouldRead: Anyone who negotiates — which is everyone
Business professionals, lawyers, diplomats, and managers
New negotiators wanting a foundational framework
whoShouldSkip: Hardball negotiators who reject collaborative approaches
